title: Trump says US strikes hit Iran in 'honour' of American soldiers killed
description: Elsewhere in the region, Kuwait's armed forces said they had intercepted Iranian drones.On the US strikes, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency rep
body: Elsewhere in the region, Kuwait's armed forces said they had intercepted Iranian drones.On the US strikes, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that several cities had been hit including Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini.There were also Iranian state media reports that two oil tankers had exploded as the vessels tried to cross the strait.The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said the tankers were going through what it called an "unsafe and hazardous southern route" of the strait and "exploded and were brought to a halt". Centcom disputed the claims. The IRGC added that the strait "will not be safe for the transit of petrochemical products, nor even a single drop of oil and gas" as long as the US strikes continue and that it will respond with a "punitive operation".Over the weekend, the US confirmed that a service member had been killed after an Iranian attack in northern Iraq on Saturday.That news came after the US said a separate attack on a base in Jordan on Friday had killed two soldiers there. The two have now been identified by the Pentagon as Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25. A third serviceperson was declared missing after the attack in Jordan, and US officials say "unidentified remains" have been found during the search process. As the strikes continued over the weekend between the US and Iran, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said that the US "always remains open to a diplomatic solution".He also said "the world has to decide whether or not it's going to allow an international waterway to be under the control" of Iran, saying Tehran was using the crucial shipping route as leverage.His comments come as a preliminary deal made in mid-June to stop fighting has unravelled, with negotiations for a permanent peace making little progress after Trump declared the agreement was "over" on 8 July.

schema.headline: Trump says US strikes hit Iran in 'honour' of American soldiers killed

title: Andy Burnham makes ending rough sleeping his first policy pledge as prime minister
description: The government said the first steps will involve helping those sleeping rough for a long period of time to move into secure housing, and includes the
body: The government said the first steps will involve helping those sleeping rough for a long period of time to move into secure housing, and includes the "acquisition of suitable homes".Burnham, in a statement after his first speech in Downing Street, said: "On my first day as prime minister, I'm announcing this government's commitment to finally end long-term rough sleeping across the UK at the earliest opportunity."We'll get to work immediately, bringing together national and local government with the investment needed to get it done."The latest annual national statistics, published by the government in February this year, estimated there were 4,793 people sleeping rough on a single night in autumn 2025 in England.This was a new record high and the fourth annual increase in a row.The number of people seen sleeping rough on a single night in Greater Manchester was recorded as 268 in 2017, 149 in 2023 and 154 in autumn 2024.The Manchester Mill reported, external that the figure had increased to 197 in 2025.Burnham visited homelessness charity The Passage in Victoria, London, hours before he made his first speech in Downing Street.Challenged about his record, Burnham said the "problem was down significantly" from when he first became mayor of Greater Manchester.He told broadcasters: "We ran a scheme every night, 600 beds, called 'A Bed Every Night' and we funded it through various different ways. But we were the only place that was doing it. "And you won't be able to get the numbers down if you're the only place and other cities, people are coming to Manchester. "So it taught me that the only way is to do this together."Burnham referenced the Everyone In scheme during the Covid-19 pandemic, when thousands of rough sleepers across England were helped with housing.He said: "Everybody pulling in the same direction, everyone in, as we did in the pandemic, because we did bring people inside in the pandemic."And that's what I'm saying as I go into government today. We did it then, we can do it now, get the basics right, put a care for people at the heart of everything that we do and just get this country working as it should again."Burnham's pledge echoes one made by former Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn, who committed to providing 8,000 homes "immediately" to house people with a history of rough sleeping in the event of the party winning power.

schema.headline: Andy Burnham makes ending rough sleeping his first policy pledge as prime minister

title: World Cup 2026: Why David Beckham was face of the tournament in the US
description: In America, nobody sells football quite like David Beckham.During this World Cup, the former England captain has been impossible to miss.On the pitch,
body: In America, nobody sells football quite like David Beckham.During this World Cup, the former England captain has been impossible to miss.On the pitch, the tournament belongs to stars like Kylian Mbappe, Jude Bellingham, Lamine Yamal and Lionel Messi.Off the pitch, Sir David has become the face of the World Cup, fronting advertising campaigns for 11 brands ranging from McDonald's and Major League Soccer to Stella Artois, Home Depot and Bank of America.And if you haven't seen him during one of the many half-time commercial breaks, you've probably spotted him at the United States' opening match in Los Angeles, watching alongside his friend Tom Cruise.Just hours before that, Beckham had become the first professional footballer to receive a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, with actor Cruise once again by his side.More recently, he was seen singing Wonderwall and Hey Jude as he celebrated Jude Bellingham's two goals against Norway that sent England into the semi-finals."David speaks to so many different people. I think that's what's unique about him," said Stacy Jones, founder and CEO of the marketing agency Hollywood Branded."He brings in the female demographic because he is so sexy - but he appeals to men. He's an honest, good, authentic, down-to-earth type of individual. So you don't really get affronted by him."She felt Beckham had achieved something few celebrities manage, moving effortlessly between football, fashion, entertainment, business and the cult of celebrity.On Hermosa Beach, south of Los Angeles, hundreds of fans gathered to watch Spain reach the World Cup final at an event organised by LA Galaxy - the club Beckham joined in 2007 from Real Madrid.Ask fans what comes to mind when they hear the name David Beckham and the answers come quickly."Bending it.""His free-kicks.""Victoria Beckham.""Handsome.""The Spice Girls.""The McDonald's advert.""David Beckham was a really good player for England, but he's doing too many commercials now," said one football fan.For Megan Hapeman, it's the recent McDonald's advert in which Beckham hopes to receive a cup featuring his own face.Her six-year-old son Oliver proudly told me he still has one."When I got it, I was pretty proud," he said. "Because Mommy liked it, I liked it too."That's exactly what brands are buying.Beckham transcends clubs, generations and even football itself.That broad appeal didn't happen by accident. It was built on Beckham's football legacy in America.He remains the player with the trademark free-kicks, part of Manchester United's Treble-winning side in 1999, England captain and one of Real Madrid's original Galácticos.Dan Courtemanche, Major League Soccer's executive vice-president and chief communications officer, admitted Beckham's influence on the game in America cannot be overstated."I always tell people he changed the fate of American soccer. Not once, but twice," he said.When Beckham signed for LA Galaxy 19 years ago, stadiums across Major League Soccer sold out before he had even played a competitive match.The league needed credibility and he gave it something even broader: mainstream celebrity.Sixteen years later, Beckham helped persuade Argentina icon Messi to choose Inter Miami over rival offers globally - another moment that transformed MLS, according to Courtemanche."Lionel Messi decided to make Major League Soccer his league of choice and Inter Miami his club of choice," he added. "Again, it took us to a whole other level."Tom Braun, LA Galaxy's president and chief operating officer, said Beckham's influence has stretched way beyond football."David's an iconic figure in our sport and our culture," he explained."He's built a business around his brand and continues to resonate with people."The evolution of the 51-year-old, who also played for AC Milan and Paris St-Germain before retiring in 2013, was perhaps best symbolised this summer on Hollywood Boulevard.When Beckham accepted his star on the Walk of Fame, he reflected he had never imagined "a working-class English footballer" would receive such an honour.The recognition celebrated not only the life of Beckham the footballer, but Beckham the cultural figure."David Beckham, to America, is the World Cup," Jones said."He is soccer to us."Having arrived in Los Angeles promising to grow the game, Beckham is still helping sell football to America.Only now, he's doing it not with a free-kick, but with a brand.

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Why David Beckham was face of the tournament in the US

title: Ryanair profits drop as Iran war puts off passengers and lifts fuel costs
description: Ryanair's profits have fallen sharply as war in the Middle East sent jet fuel prices soaring and customers reluctant to book flights.The Irish airline
body: Ryanair's profits have fallen sharply as war in the Middle East sent jet fuel prices soaring and customers reluctant to book flights.The Irish airline's pre-tax profits dropped 34% to €593m (£503m) between April and June while sales were flat as the company was forced to cut fares to stimulate demand.Ryanair also said it expects summer fares to be slightly lower than last year due to "consumer hesitancy" around air travel.The price of fuelling a plane has jumped since the US and Israel launched strikes against Iran in February and while Ryanair said it had "hedged" or struck deals for the most future fuel costs, those not included in these arrangements had more than doubled.  Crude oil prices hit $90 (£67) a barrel for the first time in a month on Monday, before falling back slightly, after a weekend of intense exchanges of fire between the US and Iran.Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz — an essential route for global oil and gas supplies — has ground to a halt. An interim peace deal last month brought some respite to oil and energy prices, but they spiked again as negotiations broke down and fighting resumed.The airline warned that its results for the year will be "highly sensitive" to external factors such as conflict escalation in the Middle East and Ukraine as well as the price of unhedged jet fuel. Fares for for the key summer period between July and September are on track to be "modestly" lower than last year, with many passengers booking closer to departure than normal.The firm's finance chief, Neil Sorahan, said flights on its popular Mediterranean routes were still full. "People [are] as keen to get away as ever, albeit booking just a little bit later," he said.Between April and June, Ryanair's revenue ticked up 1% to €4.4bn.While passenger numbers rose 6% to 6.1 million- helped by the Easter holiday in April - fares fell by 6% as the airline reduced fares to entice flyers concerned about the Iran war.Ryanair's share price fell 5% on Monday.Russ Mould, investment director at AJ Bell, said Ryanair was in a better position than many of its rivals, but nonetheless "visibility is worse than San Francisco airport when the fog sets in"."The renewed escalation in hostilities in the Middle East is unhelpful and without a lasting resolution, challenging times for the airline and travel space look set to continue."

schema.headline: Ryanair profits drop as Iran war puts off passengers and lifts fuel costs

title: JD Vance says wife Usha and son 'happy and healthy' after birth of fourth child
description: US Vice-President JD Vance has announced the birth of his fourth child, a boy, with his wife Usha Vance.Alec Neel Vance was born on Sunday morning, th
body: US Vice-President JD Vance has announced the birth of his fourth child, a boy, with his wife Usha Vance.Alec Neel Vance was born on Sunday morning, the vice-president said in a statement on social media. “Usha and the baby are happy and healthy, and our kids are overjoyed to meet their little brother,” he added, also thanking the military doctors and staff at Walter Reed Medical Center for their care. It is the first time a baby has been born to a sitting vice-president while in office for more than 150 years, according to the Associated Press. President Donald Trump hailed the news, writing in a post: "Congratulations! A perfect baby boy for the wonderful Vance family." The pair have three other children: Ewan, 9, Vivek, 6, and four-year-old Mirabel.Usha Vance (née Chilukuri) was born and raised in San Diego, California, to a mechanical engineer father and a molecular biologist mother who had moved to the US from Andhra Pradesh, India.She met JD Vance as a student at Yale Law School in 2010, when they joined a discussion group on "social decline in white America".

schema.headline: JD Vance says wife Usha and son 'happy and healthy' after birth of fourth child

title: The Open 2026: How fast Fox trumped divisive DeChambeau to win at Royal Birkdale
description: It was a joy to walk all 18 holes of Sunday's final round with Fox and the time-efficient Burns playing at such a brisk pace."See shot, hit shot. That
body: It was a joy to walk all 18 holes of Sunday's final round with Fox and the time-efficient Burns playing at such a brisk pace."See shot, hit shot. That's always been my philosophy," Fox told BBC Sport while clutching the Claret Jug.Of course, the new champion is exceptional and it would be wholly unrealistic to expect other players to compete at his pace.But there are plenty of slowcoaches for whom the prescribed 40 seconds (50 if you are first to play) allowed for hitting is often ignored. The game could do with them trying harder to get closer to Fox's example."I played with Scottie Scheffler earlier in the year," said Fox. "At Phoenix, and we're in a three-ball on the Saturday."We walk off the ninth tee, and he goes, Foxy, it doesn't even feel like you're in the group. It just feels like we're playing in a two-ball, you hit it so fast."Fox contends it is not difficult for him to play efficiently. "I just see the shot quickly," he said. "Pick a shot quickly, and the less time I have to think about it, the less bad stuff can creep in there."Potentially damaging doubts were held at bay in weekend rounds of 62 and 68 capped by the quick-fire putt that sealed the championship.It was the first time since Tom Watson in 1977 that a 72nd green birdie had secured golf's oldest and grandest title by a single shot.This was quite the moment to elevate the sun-kissed event above the DeChambeau controversy which had threatened to overshadow the week.Views varied among players as to whether he had breached Rule 8.1 by being heavy-footed around the area of his ball in the long grass to the right of the fifth hole during Friday's second round.Several American golfers stood up for the LIV player, prime among them Scheffler, who insisted that DeChambeau is "not a cheater".But this was not an issue over whether he deliberately cheated. The issue was whether he had broken a rule and officials deemed that it was a "clear-cut" breach.DeChambeau's angry reaction, delaying signing his card and therefore denying the rest of the field knowledge of their Saturday tee times was not a good look.And there was a report that DeChambeau threatened to get US President Donald Trump involved in his 30-minute conversation with rules officials. The friends have played golf together and Trump had already been involved in one high-profile sporting controversy in July, when he controversially asked football governing body Fifa to review United States striker Folarin Balogun's one-match suspension at the World Cup.Balogun's ban was suspended, allowing him to play in their last-16 defeat by Belgium.When I asked R&A chief executive Mark Darbon on Saturday if Trump had been in touch, he replied that he had not.DeChambeau continues to divide opinion. His legions of fans will feel he was hard done by and it was remarkable to witness the warm reception he received on Saturday after he had been docked those two shots.He does things his own way and there are times, like last week, when that makes him appear self-absorbed - a trait that does not usually play well in a golf locker room.Rory McIlroy made it abundantly clear, even if saying that he is "not fond" of the two time US Open champion is probably a considerable understatement.DeChambeau blanked the media all week despite being in contention and in the spotlight.Perhaps we will hear from him at LIV's event at the JCB Club in Staffordshire later this week, when the reporting contingent will be significantly smaller. Or maybe his views will be kept for his self curated YouTube channel.He is as unique as his uniform length clubs and undoubtedly brings much needed character to the game.But so too do figures such as Fox. Yes, he comes from New Zealand sporting royalty, the son of All Blacks legend Grant Fox and grandson of former Kiwi cricket captain Merv Wallace but he is a self made star.The new Open champion was 24 when he turned professional and has become a major winner only after going through the hard yards of an unforgiving pro game."My second year on the Aussie Tour, I was pretty close to giving it up," Fox said. "I remember getting to the Aussie Masters at Royal Melbourne."It was the second-to-last event of the year. I had just missed the cut at the Aussie PGA badly. My dad came over and caddied for me, and he said to me, why do you play this game?"And I said, well, it's supposed to be fun. But it wasn't really very fun at the time. He goes, well, let's just go out this week and try to have some fun."They did. Fox finished fifth, kept his card and clung on to a path that brought wins on the DP World Tour, PGA Tour and now the Open Championship.In contrast to DeChambeau, he is a unanimously popular figure among his peers. And his pacy brand of golf is great fun to watch.

schema.headline: The Open 2026: How fast Fox trumped divisive DeChambeau to win at Royal Birkdale

title: Rare identical quadruplets born in Australia
description: An Australian woman has given birth to rare, naturally-conceived identical quadruplets after a pregnancy described as "extremely high risk" by the Roy
body: An Australian woman has given birth to rare, naturally-conceived identical quadruplets after a pregnancy described as "extremely high risk" by the Royal Brisbane and Women's Hospital.The four girls, who were delivered via caesarean section at 28 weeks and four days on 14 July, were monozygotic quadruplets - where a single fertilised egg splits into four embryos.The hospital said the girls' mother, 34-year-old Jenitar Na'amoana, and her husband Jortham, already had four children and were taken by "immense surprise" when told to expect quadruplets.Dr Alexa Bendall, who cared for the mother from the start of her pregnancy, estimated identical quadruplets occur "at one in every 15 million pregnancies" and said "but to share the same placenta - this is unheard of".Bendall, who is a maternal foetal medicine specialist and obstetrician, said such a pregnancy was very high-risk, with the hospital admitting Na'amoana as an inpatient at 25 weeks so she could be monitored."We always said that if we could get her to 28 weeks, we would be doing well," Bendall said."So the fact that we've got four beautiful, healthy babies born at 28 weeks and four days is incredible and we're very happy to be a part of it," she added.

schema.headline: Rare identical quadruplets born in Australia

title: Welsh Water tells Pontycymer residents to pay £7,000 to fix water pipe leak
description: Residents say they could face prosecution if they do not pay thousands of pounds to repair a water pipe outside their homes.The residents of five hous
body: Residents say they could face prosecution if they do not pay thousands of pounds to repair a water pipe outside their homes.The residents of five houses in Pontycymer, Bridgend county, said they were told by Welsh Water that a leak from the shared pipe supplying their homes needs to be repaired at their own cost, which they estimate could cost more than £7,000 each.The Garw Valley locals claim they have been given no evidence of where the leak is which their insurance companies need to cover the cost, causing sleepless nights. A Welsh Water spokesperson said it does not own or manage the pipe so it's the responsibility of property owners, and residents have 11 days to fix it.Welsh Water added that the leak needed to be repaired as it was wasting water, and it could issue a potential prosecution under Section 75 Notice under the Water Industry Act 1991, external.The estimated cost of more than £7,000 would go towards replacing the rear water pipe in their back gardens with a new pipe at the front of the terraced row, they said, along with connecting it to each house individually.Resident Nerys Walters said the dispute had brought her massive amounts of stress over the last two years.She said they initially thought the cost of repairs could be covered through insurance, but with no evidence of the leak being provided, insurers have said they would not pay out.Walters said: "It's been a nightmare situation for us that has really caused us all a lot of stress. "Welsh Water have said they would pay for a new line that runs parallel to the front of the property, but we would then have to fund the cost of it coming in from the gate, and through the house to our rear stopcock.Walters said she had spoken to "numerous contractors" who quoted the residents more than £1,000 a metre for the work."This has been going on for about a year and a half now and we've been asking for that evidence ever since but have been given nothing", she said."No evidence of the leak, and no reason as to why they adopted other pipes in the street but not ours. All the while the deadline they have given runs down. It's been very difficult and we just want to get it sorted."Bridgend councillor Martyn Jones said it was a "distressing" time for the Pontycymer residents.Jones said he had met with an engineer from Welsh Water, on behalf of the residents but claimed he was "yet to see any evidence that one or more householders are liable"."The course of action currently being undertaken by Dwr Cymru concerns me in that I question how many other residents living in the Garw Valley and beyond are equally vulnerable if a similar situation was to develop elsewhere."Enforcement and prosecution should be the last resort. I appeal to Dwr Cymru to reconsider its position and work with local residents especially those who are financially vulnerable," he added.A Welsh Water spokesperson said: "We have been working with the residents at Gwaun Bant, Pontycymer, Bridgend, who have a leak on a private, shared pipe to their properties."The pipe is not owned or managed by Dwr Cymru Welsh Water and is the responsibility of the property owners. However, to support the residents we have offered a free connection to our main if each property connects individually."We appreciate this will be an inconvenience for customers so we have also offered a contribution towards the cost of the required work to get a pipe from the properties to the main."

schema.headline: Welsh Water tells Pontycymer residents to pay £7,000 to fix water pipe leak

title: Who is Andy Burnham's wife, Marie-France van Heel?
description: Image source, Kin Cheung/PA MediaImage caption, Quite a moment for Marie-France van Heel, as she steps into No 10 for the first time since her husband
body: Image source, Kin Cheung/PA MediaImage caption, Quite a moment for Marie-France van Heel, as she steps into No 10 for the first time since her husband became prime ministerByJennifer McKiernanPolitical reporterUpdated 1 hour agoShe was described as the "coolest girl in college" when she first started dating Andy Burnham.More than three decades later, Marie-France van Heel has walked into Downing Street alongside her husband to take on one of the most powerful unofficial roles in government - the prime minister's spouse.Burnham has replaced Sir Keir Starmer in No 10, and he met Dutch-born van Heel, known as Frankie, when she started studying at Fitzwilliam College at the University of Cambridge in 1989.He was in his second year of an English Literature degree, and Burnham's former lecturer, John Mullan, wrote in the Times that other students thought he had scooped the pools when they began dating.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Van Heel and Burnham during his 2017 Greater Manchester mayor campaignVan Heel was born in January 1970, the same month as Burnham, and grew up in the Netherlands and Belgium before moving to the UK.Although they started dating at university, van Heel had a surprising request in the early days of their relationship, when she asked Burnham if she could go on Cilla Black's TV dating show Blind Date - and he agreed.So in 1992, wearing flares, a bottle green jacket and a yellow headband over her bobbed blonde hair, van Heel appeared on the hit ITV programme.She chose Will from Surrey to be her date on a holiday to Gibraltar, but the pair did not hit it off.Having quickly reunited, Burnham and van Heel were reportedly in a bar in Parliament 12 years later when Will walked in, having become the director of communications for the Conservative Party. Image source, Shutterstock/ ITVImage caption, Van Heel appeared on the ITV show Blind Date, hosted by Cilla Black, in 1992By this point, van Heel had given birth to their first child, Jimmy - and it was his birth in March 2000 which Burnham has said spurred them on to get married, after years of living together."I think marriage is better for kids," Burnham told the Daily Telegraph in 2007, external, adding: "I'm a bit old fashioned on these matters - although I have to admit that Jimmy, my eldest son, was at our wedding so I'm only old fashioned up to a point. "When my wife got pregnant for the first time it was a complete shock. My first thought was, 'Oh my God, we'll have to get married'."Their daughters, Rosie and Annie, were born a few years later while the couple were living in Manchester.Rosie and Jimmy work for drinks brand Jägermeister and the Royal College of Nursing respectively, while Annie has just graduated from Cardiff University. Neither van Heel nor the children are expected to move into Downing Street, but nothing has been confirmed about where their rescue dog Axel will live. Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Andy Burnham and Marie-France van Heel have been together since their university daysImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Burnham celebrates winning the Greater Manchester mayoral election in 2017 with son Jimmy (L) wife Marie-France van Heel (2nd-L) daughter Rosie (3rd-L) and daughter Annie (4th-R) and his mum Eileen (R).Van Heel has worked in marketing and strategy for many years, notably for Sky, and has also been involved in the logo designs for the BBC and England Rugby.That professional experience is credited by some for helping design Burnham's striking campaign logos, external, which feature a cartoon of the northerner in his trademark black t-shirt and black NHS-style glasses.There has also been a small controversy over van Heel's current role as the chief marketing officer of Iduna, which owns large parts of Manchester's electric vehicle charging network.As Greater Manchester Mayor, Burnham had declared his wife's job and stepped away from all decisions about Iduna and any linked infrastructure to avoid any perception of a conflict of interest.The Conservatives complained he had not declared she held 0.2% of company shares, the Times , externalreported, but the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) confirmed Burnham was given legal advice that no further declaration was necessary because he had already declared Van Heel as an employee."We are satisfied that the mayor has met his legal obligations to register disclosable pecuniary interests," a GCMA spokesperson said.

schema.headline: Who is Andy Burnham's wife, Marie-France van Heel?

title: Edward Stourton: Broadcaster and journalist dies aged 68
description: BySteven McIntoshEntertainment reporterBroadcaster and journalist Edward Stourton, whose career spanned more than 40 years including a decade on Radio
body: BySteven McIntoshEntertainment reporterBroadcaster and journalist Edward Stourton, whose career spanned more than 40 years including a decade on Radio 4's Today, has died aged 68. Stourton, who had been diagnosed with prostate cancer in 2014, died on Saturday surrounded by his family, the BBC announced in a statement. The presenter's friend and former Today co-host James Naughtie praised Stourton's "warm intelligence and wit", adding he was "one of the best colleagues". Another former Today presenter, Evan Davis, said Stourton was "a very important figure for all of us on the daily news programmes on Radio 4"."One of those people you're really glad to have around," Davis reflected. "All substance, minimum ego, deeply knowledgeable, totally reliable."And he was one of the nicest of colleagues as well. He'll be missed."Presenter Mishal Husain described Stourton as "a true gentleman and a broadcaster of immense capacity and intellect", while Justin Webb remembered him as "funny, self-deprecating and unpompous".Figure caption, Jim Naughtie reflects on the life and legacy of broadcaster Edward Stourton, who has died aged 68.In his 2023 memoir Confessions, Stourton revealed he had been diagnosed with prostate cancer nearly a decade earlier.He had treatment but was later told it had become incurable and spread. Stourton continued to work until very recently.Stourton's career began at ITN, where he helped launch Channel 4 News and became the programme's first Washington correspondent in the latter years of the Reagan presidency.He joined the BBC as Paris correspondent in 1988, and after a period back at ITN as diplomatic editor, returned to present the One O'Clock News on BBC One. His BBC work also included Panorama, Assignment, Correspondent, as well as a phone-in programme on Radio 4, Call Ed Stourton.Stourton became a presenter of the flagship Today programme in 1999, where his immense experience as a foreign correspondent served him well while reporting from countries such as Lebanon and Afghanistan.He was also one of the first correspondents to reach New York after the 9/11 terror attacks.Image caption, Stourton in 2007 with Today co-hosts Carolyn Quinn, John Humphrys, Sarah Montague and James NaughtieHis departure from Today in 2008 was leaked to a newspaper before he had been told, upsetting Stourton and many of his colleagues.But he remained at the BBC and went on to present several other news programmes, while remaining as a contributor to Today.In recent years, as well as writing and presenting documentaries for Radio 4, Stourton fronted the network's religious affairs programme, Sunday, as well as regularly hosting The World At One and The World This Weekend.Image caption, Stourton's time at the BBC included working on Correspondent, which took him to KosovoStourton was born in 1957 in Nigeria, where his father was working, and also spent time as a boy in Geneva. He was educated at Ampleforth College and read English Literature at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was President of the Union.His Catholic faith was an important part of his life and Stourton fronted documentaries including Absolute Truth, a four-part BBC Two series about the Catholic Church.It was also reflected in his writing - among Stourton's many books was John Paul II: Man of History, a revelatory biography of the former Pope. His last book, written to coincide with the 250th anniversary of the American Declaration of Independence, was Made in America: the Dark History that Led to Donald Trump.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Edward Stourton photographed at home in 2003Paying tribute, Naughtie praised Stourton's "great integrity and humanity", adding that he "had a kind of infectious decency which lifted people up"."He had a great effect on younger producers, who came away with enormous respect for the extent to which he tried to pass things on," Naughtie added."He was a man of wide cultural interests and experience and enthusiasm... His warm intelligence and wit were admired and enjoyed by generations with whom he worked."And as a friend, he was always a rock of support and kindness. His dignity and courage through his illness were an inspiration to everyone who knew him. He was, simply, much-loved."The Today programme's current editor Rebecca Keating said she and colleagues referred to Stourton as the "Rolls Royce of broadcasting"."His sharp mind and vast knowledge of the world brought to bear on any story with zero fuss," she wrote on X.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Stourton pictured at the Edinburgh International Book Festival at Charlotte Square Gardens in 2018The BBC's interim news chief Jonathan Munro said it was "far too soon to lose Ed"."I worked as his producer when he was ITN's diplomatic editor - unflappable then and ever after," he continued."Many years later, I was listening to his superb analysis of the new Pope for the BBC - another of his many areas of expertise. Not many broadcasters can present Today, BBC One's One O'clock News, The World at One and Sunday - but he did. He leaves a huge gap."Today presenter Nick Robinson said Stourton "brought a ferocious intellect, boundless curiosity and a good deal of charm to all he did". "He dealt with his cancer with resolution but without self pity," Robinson added. "He'll be badly missed."Radio 4 controller Mohit Bakaya described Stourton as an "absolutely outstanding broadcaster", adding that he "brought intelligence, authority and an extraordinary hinterland to everything he did".ITV's Julie Etchingham, who also presented Radio 4's Sunday programme, said she was "very sad to hear the news" of Stourton's death, remembering his "huge intellect and great heart".Stourton, who had homes in south London and south-west France, is survived by his wife Fiona, who is a senior television producer, two sons, a daughter and a step-daughter.

schema.headline: Edward Stourton: Broadcaster and journalist dies aged 68

title: Are you a parent missing out on £27 a week? Here's how to check
description: Thousands more parents could receive £27 a week by remembering some paperwork shortly after their baby is born.Child benefit is an allowance that help
body: Thousands more parents could receive £27 a week by remembering some paperwork shortly after their baby is born.Child benefit is an allowance that helps with the cost of bringing up children. It is currently worth £27.05 a week for the first child, and £17.90 a week for each subsequent child.Official data shows that parents of nearly a third of children eligible for child benefit do not make a claim before their baby's first birthday.Payments can only be backdated for three months, meaning some families could be missing out on hundreds of pounds in the fog of new parenthood.The annual publication of the most popular baby names could serve as a reminder to some parents that there are other pieces of official paperwork to complete.In order to receive child benefit, which is paid every four weeks, one parent should make a claim, external. That parent will also get National Insurance credits towards their state pension.To claim, you need: Your child's birth or adoption certificateYour bank detailsThe National Insurance number for you and your partner, if you have oneChild Benefit can be claimed 48 hours after the baby's birth is registered but can only be backdated for up to three months from the date HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) receives the claim.More than 6.8 million parents received child benefit in the year to August 2025, according to HMRC data, external. But only 69% of them claimed the support before their baby's first birthday, meaning thousands of families may not getting what they are entitled to receive.The tax authority said that many of them may have chosen not to claim, owing to restrictions on high-earning parents receiving the benefit.Under the High Income Child Benefit Charge, external, payments start to reduce if one parent earns £60,000 a year. They stop entirely when one of you earns £80,000.

schema.headline: Are you a parent missing out on £27 a week? Here's how to check

title: Hope Street: The small seaside town winning big from TV tourism
description: Kathryn Strain, who is a member of the Chunky Dunkers sea swimming group in Donaghadee, says it's common to see tourists from cruise ships visiting Be
body: Kathryn Strain, who is a member of the Chunky Dunkers sea swimming group in Donaghadee, says it's common to see tourists from cruise ships visiting Belfast making trips to the town."You can hear them saying, 'oh, that's where the police station was, and oh, this is so and so's house'," so it has been great, it has been good for the town, definitely," she told BBC News NI.Some of the Hope Street cast and crew have also braved the cold of Irish Sea and enjoyed - or maybe not - a dip.Like many locals, Kathryn can be spotted in Hope Street episodes as an extra."I think my most famous [part] was, and as my husband says I've got the face for a funeral, I was in it at a funeral," she jokes.Her friend, Bridie McElhill, who also swims with the Chunky Dunkers, says the show has helped put Donaghadee "on the map"."People come to Donaghadee now to see the town and see all the locations where the filming has taken place."I do think it has brought people into the town, and that's great for the town."

schema.headline: Hope Street: The small seaside town winning big from TV tourism

title: Marcus Rashford: What does the future hold for Manchester United forward?
description: Marcus Rashford's World Cup ended with a considered and thoughtful social media post, issued in the aftermath of England's amazing 6-4 third-place pla
body: Marcus Rashford's World Cup ended with a considered and thoughtful social media post, issued in the aftermath of England's amazing 6-4 third-place play-off victory over France in Miami.Disappointments are guaranteed in football, he said, before opening out in a more positive vein and stating firmly: "Keep believing in us. Our time will come."He added: "Now it's time for me to rest, reflect, spend some quality time with family and friends, and come back a better version of myself."'Reflect' is the big word in that overview.At 28, Rashford is moving into what should be the prime years of his career.He has just won his first league title, La Liga with Barcelona, has 77 England caps and featured in six out of eight games as Thomas Tuchel's side delivered the country's best World Cup performance since 1966.But in reality, a large part of what Rashford will be reflecting on with his family - some of whom are also key figures in terms of guiding his career - is whether his club future lies at Manchester United.Rashford is contracted to Manchester United until 2028. His salary of £325,000 a week makes him the club's top earner at a time when minority owner Sir Jim Ratcliffe is trying to stay on top of salary costs, which are, by some distance, the club's highest expenditure.Barcelona had an option, in the loan deal they agreed for Rashford last season, to turn it into a permanent transfer for £26m. As expected, that deadline passed in the middle of June without it being activated.There was another clause in Rashford's contract that allowed other clubs - Liverpool and Manchester City excepted - to buy him for £40m, which expired earlier in July. As expected, no-one triggered that either.Sources close to the player felt it was illogical to think a club would come in and offer more than Barcelona would have had to pay.With England's World Cup campaign at an end, Rashford is now entitled to a three-week break.Unless the timings are altered, or his situation changes, he will return to Manchester United just as they begin a six-day training camp in the Republic of Ireland, scheduled around an historic meeting with old rivals Leeds at Croke Park on 12 August.Suggestions around Rashford have been that he is relaxed about the prospect of returning to training at United.For a start, there is not the same confrontational relationship between the player and his coach.Ruben Amorim fell out with the forward within weeks of his arrival from Sporting Lisbon. Amorim eventually exiled him, which triggered a loan move to Aston Villa. Then the head coach put Rashford in his 'bomb squad', along with Alejandro Garnacho, Antony, Tyrell Malacia and Jadon Sancho, when pre-season training began last summer. The other four players have all now left the club.Amorim's replacement, Michael Carrick, is a former team-mate of Rashford's. He has also been his coach and was, briefly, his manager, following Ole Gunnar Solskjaer's dismissal in 2021. The pair are said to get on well.Carrick's only public statement around Rashford, towards the end of last season, was conciliatory in nature, which is in keeping with his general personality. He is so far yet to be asked what his stance is around the player returning to the fold.For their part, United are sticking to their refusal to renegotiate a deal with Barcelona on reduced terms or consider a second loan to the Catalan giants. It is not clear what interest there is in Rashford at the Nou Camp given Barca have already signed England wide-man Anthony Gordon and are about to bring in Germany forward Karim Adeyemi from Borussia Dortmund.The consistency of United's messaging is being articulated in a summer where they are keen to prove they will not be bullied into overpaying to get deals over the line. That financial discipline may have consequences - while the feeling was Elliot Anderson would have joined Manchester City anyway, United did not countenance paying the £116m Nottingham Forest eventually sold him for.The same is true of Mateus Fernandes and his £85m switch from West Ham to Tottenham.Nevertheless, Manchester United's enhanced used of data through director Michael Sansoni and his dedicated team means the club feel bullish about their chances of landing alternative targets who offer better value.Clearly just as United need active sellers to buy from, they need buyers so they can offload those players they do not want.In Rashford's case, there are a limited number of clubs globally who could match his current salary. And where a club are interested, the player has to want to join them.Judging by his recent social media post, he would not do anything to jeopardise his England place. BBC Sport has also been told of some Premier League clubs he would not be keen to move to.So where will Rashford be when the transfer window closes on 1 September? Currently, the likeliest answer, by some distance, is Manchester United.After that: Any Spanish club, any Turkish club, any German club. The vagueness there underlines the current lack of market for the 15th highest scorer in United's history, with 138 goals - someone who is only 17 short of a place in the top 10.There are plenty, including former captain Gary Neville, who feel Rashford's time at Old Trafford is over and there should be no reconciliation.In the end though, it may have to happen - and not everyone would be disappointed."A fit and focused Marcus Rashford has the ability to be one of the best players," said Rashford's friend Sam Smith - the Wrexham forward who scored the winner in their pre-season victory over Carrick's team in Helsinki on Saturday - when speaking to the United We Stand podcast. "He has everything."He has had a difficult time at United and getting away from it all was a good thing."I don't know what is going to happen but as his friend, I hope he comes back and proves how good a player he was, how good he can be and starts scoring goals again."

schema.headline: Marcus Rashford: What does the future hold for Manchester United forward?

title: World Cup 2026: Could Gianni Infantino push through a 64-team tournament?
description: If we have learned one thing from 10 years of Gianni Infantino as Fifa president, it is that more is better.More tournaments, more games, more money.T
body: If we have learned one thing from 10 years of Gianni Infantino as Fifa president, it is that more is better.More tournaments, more games, more money.The new-look Club World Cup, the expanded World Cup, the inflated ticket prices.And, of course, the Super Bowl-style show that meant the half-time break at the World Cup final lasted 27 minutes and 22 seconds.But why stop there? Infantino appears to have thrown the door wide open to a 64-team World Cup."If you don't give smaller countries a chance to participate, they'll lack the incentive to keep improving," he said.Except, of course, smaller countries are not denied the chance. If they are good enough, they qualify. See Cape Verde, Curacao, Jordan and Uzbekistan.Purely in terms of the competition format, it does make sense. The top two teams in the 16 groups would go through to the knockout stage. No longer would there be a need to send a few third-placed teams through.But is there really any other logical reason to expand to 64 teams?Let's start with how we got here.The 2030 World Cup will mark 100 years since the first World Cup, which was held in Uruguay.The tournament will primarily be hosted by Morocco, Portugal and Spain - but the opening three matches will be played in Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay as centenary hosts.Conmebol - the South American confederation - wants more games.In April 2025, it requested a one-off tournament of 64 teams so it could take on the full programme of the additional four groups.Its president, Alejandro Dominguez, said it would ensure "nobody on the planet is left out of the party".There has been little support from elsewhere.Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin dismissed it as a "bad idea" for both the tournament and qualifying process.Victor Montagliani - president of the governing body of North and Central America and the Caribbean (Concacaf) - said last year it would damage "the broader football ecosystem".Asian Football Confederation (AFC) president Sheikh Salman bin Ibrahim Al Khalifa agreed, saying further expansion could bring "chaos".None of which means it will not happen, with the decision taken by the 37 members of the Fifa Council.A 64-team tournament has two main plus points: a more natural format, and more opportunities for emerging nations.It is the latter which Infantino is championing, especially after the heroics of Cape Verde. Fifa faced criticism before the tournament, with suggestions the additional teams would not be competitive. Then Cape Verde drew with Spain and Uruguay to finish second in their group. And the Blue Sharks gave Argentina an almighty scare by taking them to extra time in the last 16, before losing 3-2.But the fairytale stories must be balanced with the competitive edge of the tournament. Haiti, Iraq, Jordan, Panama, Tunisia and Uzbekistan failed to pick up a point - and this summer's group stage had minimal jeopardy for the top nations.Add more teams and it would be difficult to see a repeat of top-four seeds like Belgium (2022), Germany (2018), Spain (2014), Italy (2010) and France (2002) failing to make to knockout stage.Then there is the question of where the extra places would go.Based on this year's qualifying, these are the additional countries who would have made it into a 64-team World Cup 2026 (with their Fifa ranking at the start of the tournament in brackets):AFC: Indonesia (118), Oman (79), United Arab Emirates (68).Uefa: Denmark (21), Italy (12), Poland (36).Concacaf: Honduras (65), Jamaica (71), Suriname (125).Conmebol: Bolivia (77), Venezuela (49).OFC: New Caledonia (151).Africa: Burkina Faso (62), Cameroon (44), Gabon (86), Nigeria (26).Six of those teams are from the top 50 of Fifa's world rankings - three from Europe, two from Africa and one from South America.And therein lies the problem. Asia and Concacaf are weaker confederations, as was proven by performances this year. Nine of Africa's 10 countries got out of the group stage. But only two of nine Asian countries advanced, and just three of the six Concacaf countries - the co-hosts - made it through. New Zealand, who got one point and finished bottom of their group, are the only country from Oceania in the world's top 150.There is an argument that if you extend again, you should give a greater weight of places to Europe just for competitive balance.But, on the other hand, there are those who would argue that bringing more teams into the World Cup ultimately allows the game to develop further in those countries.Infantino will sell the expansion as being good for the global game, but there have to be doubts that it is good for the competition.Almost a third of Fifa's 211 nations would be present. Would the group stage not simply become a glorified pan-confederation qualifying competition for what is, essentially, a knockout tournament? Expanding the World Cup to 64 teams is really just a case of bolting on four additional four-team groups.But more groups means more time.This year's tournament lasted 39 days. An additional 24 matches would require 20 schedule slots. Or another five days.But this summer's event was unique for two reasons: multiple time zones and indoor stadiums.Fifa was able to elongate a matchday at either end, with games scheduled over a 14-hour period so it could stay true to exclusive time windows for television coverage.That will not be so straightforward in the future, even with 48 teams.Climate change is making playing in the daytime more challenging - see the record heatwaves in Europe in recent weeks.Other countries are far less likely to have the covered venues of Atlanta, Dallas Houston and Vancouver to mitigate it.The Saudi Arabia World Cup is likely to move to January 2035 but it is not clear how other countries will get around this growing problem.Until this year, only once had there been dual hosts of the World Cup - Japan and South Korea in 2002.It is hard to imagine any one country ever being capable of hosting again. Even Saudi Arabia. Could we be looking at World Cups hosted by, for instance, the UK and Scandinavia? Or Eastern Europe?How would an Asian or African country be able to fund hosting the World Cup?Let's look a just a few issues that would need to be resolved.This summer there were 16 host cities with stadiums having capacities ranging from 43,000 to 81,000. More would be required.There would need to be 64 individual, elite-level training facilities for the qualifying nations. Then there is the provision of hotels for the fans, and the infrastructure and support network to move them around.Expanding the World Cup is not as simple as giving more opportunities to more countries.One of Infantino's key promises when he was elected was to bring more money into the game.Every football association now has access to $8m (£5.9m) in funding over a four-year period, with a further $1.2m (£890,000) on top for the smaller nations.Confederations get $60m (£44.5m) to develop, promote and organise football.It is why Infantino is likely to be re-elected unopposed next year.This money has to come from somewhere, primarily the World Cup.In 2017, Fifa projected a 48-team World Cup would bring in $6.5bn (£4.8bn).In 2022, it revised the format again and added 24 games. Now, it expects to rake in $9bn (£6.7bn) this year.A 64-team event is likely to raise more from television, sponsorship and ticket sales.Fifa says it has a responsibility to grow the game, and its tool for this appears to be more and more football.But critics would argue there has to be a point where expansion is no longer good for the game.

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Could Gianni Infantino push through a 64-team tournament?

title: Fire crews battle blaze on Arthur’s Seat in Edinburgh
description: Image source, Craig Duncan PhotographyImage caption, Police closed the road surrounding Arthur's Seat as firefighters tackled the fireUpdated 1 hour a
body: Image source, Craig Duncan PhotographyImage caption, Police closed the road surrounding Arthur's Seat as firefighters tackled the fireUpdated 1 hour agoFire crews have spent the night battling a large blaze on Arthur's Seat in Edinburgh.Small plumes of smoke could still be seen on Monday morning along Salisbury Crags after the fire broke out the previous evening.Six fire engines were sent to tackle the grassfire with firefighters seen dousing the flames.The Scottish Fire and Rescue Service (SFRS) said there were "no reported casualties" but Police Scotland said Queen's Drive, the road that encircles the Holyrood Park hills, remained closed to all vehicles.It is the second fire in the last three months at the landmark, an ancient extinct volcano that offers panoramic views of the Scottish capital.  Edinburgh University student Mahek Jayantilial, who lives near Arthur's Seat, said she could see the fire from her window."I was a bit worried because I thought the fire would come this way, but thankfully there's enough concrete here to stop it," the 24-year-old told the Press Association.Figure caption, Fire crews tackle blaze on Arthur’s Seat in EdinburghJayantilial said it was "upsetting" to see Arthur's Seat on fire but she believed it was almost unavoidable as there has not been much rain recently and the grass had dried out."It was bound to happen," she added."A small bit of glass or a poorly snuffed-out cigarette butt would have ignited it, and then something so small creates such a big fire."Large swathes of vegetation below the cliff face were burned black by the fire and the sound of chainsaws could be heard on Monday morning, believed to be from work to clear vegetation. On the ground firefighters were concentrating efforts on the area of Queen's Drive closest to the Pollock Halls of Residence, the largest student accommodation site for Edinburgh University. Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Student Mahek Jayantilial said the grass had dried out from the lack of rainfallImage source, Malcolm McFadyenImage caption, Vegetation on the hillside burned as the fire spreadJon Henderson, SFRS assistant chief officer, said the service had dealt with a number of incidents over the course of the weekend. "We had crews at Arthur's Seat on Sunday evening and then overnight," he told BBC Radio Scotland Breakfast."At first light we stepped up that response again when the conditions were favourable for that."The feeling from the ground there is that we will probably be there for a couple of days continuing to tackle it and dampen out hotspots."Henderson said it was too early to speculate on the cause of the fire.  "Our focus at the moment is dealing with the fire and the response but there will be a full fire investigation," he said. "The difficulty we have with open fires is that they can be difficult to assess where they started because of the scale and sometimes these do happen naturally."Image gallerySkip image galleryImage source, Craig Duncan PhotographyImage caption, A large plume of smoke could be seen from as far as Fife on Sunday eveningSlide 1 of 6, An aerial shot of white plumes of smoke coming from Arthur's Seat. , A large plume of smoke could be seen from as far as Fife on Sunday eveningCallum Smith, who also lives near Holyrood Park, said he was concerned about the impact the fire will have on a nearby peregrine falcon nesting site."Peregrine falcons have been nesting here for four, five, six years, possibly longer," he said.Smith said there were two adults and two chicks in a nest where the fire was along the cliffs and the youngest chick had not fledged yet."Possibly the two adults and the eldest chick would have been able to fly away, but the outcome for the youngest one is unclear," he added."Unfortunately, the smoke traversed over the top of the cliffs and onto the cliffs, so the smoke would have gone directly onto the nest site."Cairngorms wildfireIt is the latest call out for the Scottish fire service during the recent stretch of warm, dry weather which has seen a heightened risk of wildfires. Hundreds of firefighters, two helicopters and specialist resources were sent to the Cairngorms National Park where a blaze has been ongoing for four days.Six square kilometres of land had been affected resulting in homes and businesses, including campsites and a ski resort in the Glenmore Forest Park area, being evacuated as a safety precaution.A high volume pump has been used to draw water from Loch Morlich to support firefighting efforts.On Sunday, fire crews said the fire was now "surrounded" and police began escorting residents and campers back to properties and campsites to collect personal belongings.Henderson said resources remain on site but "significant progress" has been made."The activity over the rest of the day is to maintain a four kilometre long fire break and to search out and extinguish hotspots," he added.Get involvedYour news, your pictures

schema.headline: Fire crews battle blaze on Arthur’s Seat in Edinburgh
